title: A Classical Approximation Point of View on Some Results in the Spectral Theory of Jacobi Matrices
speaker: Mira Shamis
abstract: Deift--Simon and Poltoratskii--Remling proved upper bounds on the measure of the absolutely continuous spectrum of Jacobi matrices. Using methods of classical approximation theory, we give a new proof of their results, and generalize them in several ways. First, we prove a sharper inequality taking the distribution of the values of the potential into account. Second, we prove a generalization of a "local" inequality of Deift--Simon to the non-ergodic setting. Based on joint work with Sasha Sodin

proof_stages.label: Jacobi-matrix setup and AC spectral support
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines bounded half-line Jacobi matrices, their spectrum, the essential support of their absolutely continuous spectrum, and the simplifying but nonessential specialization to a_n≡1.

proof_stages.label: Global and localized AC-measure estimates
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the Deift-Simon bound |Σ_ac(J_{1,b})|≤4 and the Poltoratskii-Remling nonergodic extension, the speaker states a new interval-local estimate whose product term records how often the potential values lie far from the energy interval.

proof_stages.label: Integrated density of states form of the bound
proof_stages.summary: The finite-volume eigenvalue counting functions K_n and the integrated density of states K are introduced, and the Deift-Simon differential inequality d(-2cosπK(e))/de≥1 is shown to imply the global length bound by integration.

proof_stages.label: Periodic discriminant reduction of the global inequality
proof_stages.summary: For a q-periodic operator, transfer matrices produce the q-period discriminant Δ_q, a monic degree-q polynomial with essential spectrum {E: |Δ_q(E)|≤2}, reducing the periodic proof of |Σ_ac|≤4 to a polynomial extremal estimate.

proof_stages.label: Classical approximation engine: Pólya and Chebyshev
proof_stages.summary: The quantity L_n(K) is defined as the least sup-norm of a monic degree-n polynomial on K, and Pólya’s inequality is proved from contraction-to-an-interval, Lagrange interpolation, and Chebyshev alternation, yielding the desired measure bound when applied to the discriminant.

proof_stages.label: Removal of periodicity by subordinacy
proof_stages.summary: In the general bounded nonperiodic case, a Gilbert-Pearson subordinacy estimate controls the exponential growth of finite-block transfer polynomials on the AC spectrum, allowing the same approximation-theoretic argument to prove the star inequality without ergodicity.

proof_stages.label: Bandwise and subsequential local Deift-Simon inequality
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the periodic band-length inequality, with equality only for constant potential, and explains that the same extremal-polynomial method gives the local Deift-Simon inequality for every subsequential IDS limit, geometrically because bands grow as they are moved together toward the free-Laplacian configuration.

examples.name: Free Laplacian extremal case
examples.note: The constant-zero potential has purely absolutely continuous spectrum [-2,2] of length 4, which motivates asking for quantitative improvements when the potential takes separated values and later explains the glued-band extremal picture.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the free Laplacian concretely to identify its spectrum as [-2,2], compute the AC spectral measure as 4, and later interpret it as the glued-band extremal periodic case

passes.reason: the zero-potential constant-coefficient Jacobi operator is a highly symmetric free/base case attaining the sharp AC-measure bound four.

examples.name: Separated two-valued periodic potential
examples.note: A period m+l potential taking 0 exactly m times and r≥5 exactly l times is used to demonstrate the refined theorem, giving explicit bounds on the AC measure near [-2,2] and near [r-2,r+2].
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the two-valued periodic potential with zeros and r’s, locates the relevant intervals, computes the index counts and d_n asymptotics, and derives explicit spectral-measure bounds

examples.name: Gluing a finite union of intervals
examples.note: For the contraction step in Pólya’s inequality, a finite union of intervals is visualized by sliding the intervals together until they form one interval of the same Lebesgue measure.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes the concrete contraction construction of pushing a finite union of intervals together until they glue into one interval of the same measure

passes.reason: the gluing collapses gaps to the single-interval configuration, which is the fixed-measure extremal case in the Poltoratskii inequality argument.
Gluing removes all gaps to a single interval, the collapsed fixed-measure configuration extremal in the Poltoratskii comparison.
Gluing the finite union collapses gaps into a single same-measure interval, the extremal interval case in the polynomial inequality argument.
Gluing the intervals closes all gaps into a single interval, the limiting configuration that gives the extremal interval case in the approximation argument.
